planting.note: Direct sow on the surface of prepared, weed-free soil in spring; big bluestem seed germinates best with good soil contact. Alternatively, cold-stratify seed for 30 days before spring sowing to improve germination rate.

directions: Plant in well-drained to average, dry soil in full sun; big bluestem is the dominant grass of the tallgrass prairie, once covering hundreds of millions of acres from Manitoba to Texas, and is a cornerstone native grass for prairie restorations, meadow gardens, and large-scale naturalized plantings. The blue-green foliage turns brilliant orange-copper-burgundy in fall and holds through winter as attractive standing tan straw. The distinctive three-parted seed heads — resembling turkey feet in silhouette — are architectural and wildlife-valuable. Deep roots (up to 10 feet) make it exceptional for erosion control and sequestering carbon. Extremely drought-tolerant once established; never needs irrigation or fertilizer in average to dry soil. Cut back to 6 inches in early spring before new growth begins.

planting.note: Direct sow on prepared, weed-free soil in spring; seed can be surface-sown or barely covered at 1/8 inch depth. Fall dormant seeding (after October) is also effective — natural stratification over winter breaks dormancy.

directions: Plant in well-drained, average to dry soil in full sun; indiangrass is one of the four dominant tall-grass prairie grasses, companion to big bluestem, switchgrass, and little bluestem. Its narrow blue-green foliage and elegant golden-yellow seed plumes waving in the fall breeze make it one of the most ornamentally beautiful native grasses. Seed heads emerge in August and September with a distinctive twisted 'awns' that catch the light like bronze silk. Fall color is deep orange-gold, and standing winter stems provide bird seed and structure through snow. Like all warm-season prairie grasses, it is slow to start in spring — do not mistake lateness for death. Cut back to 4–6 inches in early spring annually.

planting.note: Direct sow on the surface of prepared soil in spring; sideoats grama germinates readily without stratification in warm soil. Also excellent as a fall-dormant sown grass — broadcast seed from October through December for natural spring germination.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; sideoats grama is the official state grass of Texas and one of the most widely adapted and drought-resistant native grasses in North America. It is called 'sideoats' for its distinctive seed heads, which hang in a row of small oat-like florets all on one side of the arching seed stalks from July through September, creating a charming, delicate appearance in late summer. The deep blue-green foliage turns orange-red in fall. Sideoats is one of the few native grasses that performs reliably in the extreme heat and drought of the Southern Plains, making it invaluable for Texas and Gulf Coast gardens with dry, alkaline soil. An excellent low-maintenance lawn substitute or turf replacement on slopes.

planting.note: Scarify seed with sandpaper and soak 24 hours before sowing; direct sow in fall for natural stratification and spring germination. Transplant potted plants in spring. Plants are slow to establish and should not be divided or moved once sited.

directions: Plant in well-drained, average to poor, even dry soil in full sun; blue wild indigo is a long-lived native prairie perennial that forms a substantial, rounded, blue-green shrub-like clump bearing stunning spikes of true-indigo-blue pea flowers in May, followed by interesting inflated, rattling seedpods that persist through winter. It is a larval host plant for several native butterfly and moth species. Choose its permanent location carefully — blue wild indigo has a deep taproot and strongly resists transplanting once established, but plants can persist for 50+ years with no maintenance. Initially slow to establish (3 years to a full-sized clump), but thereafter essentially indestructible and extremely drought-tolerant. A specimen plant that will outlast the gardener.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification; alternatively cold-stratify seed at 35–40°F for 60 days before spring sowing. Plant corms 2–3 inches deep in well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant corms 2–3 inches deep or direct sow in well-drained, average to dry soil in full sun; prairie blazingstar is one of the tallest blazingstars, producing dense, 2–3 foot spikes of bright rose-purple flowers from July through August that open from the top of the spike downward — the reverse of most flowering spikes. It is one of the premier late-summer nectar plants for monarch butterflies preparing for fall migration, as well as a magnet for native bees and swallowtails. The grass-like, basal foliage is unobtrusive early in the season; plants emerge from small corms that multiply slowly over the years. Excellent cut flower. Tolerates clay soil better than many prairie plants and adapts well to average garden conditions.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in fall for natural stratification; seed requires 60-90 days cold-moist stratification before germination. Direct sow 1/4 inch deep where plants are to grow permanently — compass plant has a deep taproot and transplants very poorly.

directions: Sow seed directly in its permanent location — deeply and well-drained, full sun; compass plant is a dramatic prairie giant, eventually producing 6–10 foot flower stalks lined with sunflower-like yellow flowers from July through September in mature plants. The deeply cut, pinnate basal leaves are oriented north-south on the plant (hence the common name), a feature used by prairie travelers for navigation. The deep taproot (reaching 15+ feet in mature plants) makes it essentially indestructible to drought and allows it to colonize harsh, rocky, and dry sites. Plants are extremely slow to bloom from seed — typically 3–5 years — but then persist for decades without any care. A cornerstone statement plant for native meadow gardens and prairie restorations.

planting.note: Direct sow on the soil surface in fall for natural cold stratification and spring germination; or cold-stratify seed 4 weeks before spring sowing. Surface sow only — seed needs light to germinate. Self-seeds freely once established.

directions: Sow on the soil surface in partial shade to full sun in moist to well-drained, average soil; wild columbine is a delicate native perennial of rocky woodlands, ledges, and open forest edges whose unique red-and-yellow nodding spurred flowers in April–June are the primary nectar source for ruby-throated hummingbirds returning from spring migration. The blue-green, lobed foliage is attractive all season. Plants self-seed prolifically and naturalize freely in appropriate conditions — allow seedlings to establish a colony rather than removing all volunteers. Short-lived (3–5 years) but self-perpetuating through seeding. Columbine leaf miners leave unsightly tunnels in foliage; tolerate minor damage or simply remove affected leaves.

planting.note: Plant small corms (bulbs) 2–3 inches deep in fall; or sow fresh seed immediately after collection in late spring, as seed viability drops quickly. Corms are the practical starting method — bulbs begin blooming in 2–3 years vs. 4–7 from seed.

directions: Plant small corms 2–3 inches deep in moist, humus-rich soil beneath deciduous trees; yellow trout lily is among the most charming spring ephemerals of the eastern forest floor. The single mottled brown-and-green leaf (resembling a brook trout's back) appears in early spring, followed by a solitary nodding yellow lily flower on plants old enough to bloom. Young plants form non-blooming single-leaf colonies for 4–7 years before maturity. Plants go completely dormant by early June, leaving no trace; mark their location to avoid disturbance. The fleshy corms are edible and were eaten by Indigenous peoples, though harvesting from wild populations is unethical. Ideal naturalized en masse under deciduous trees to create a spectacular spring carpet.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes just 1 inch deep in fall with the growing tip pointing upward; or sow fresh seed immediately after collection in June — seed viability is very brief and must be planted within days of harvest. Disturb rhizomes minimally.

directions: Plant rhizomes 1 inch deep in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade beneath deciduous trees; bloodroot is one of the earliest and most beautiful spring woodland wildflowers, producing brilliant white, poppy-like flowers (the double-flowered form 'Multiplex' looks like a white peony blossom) that emerge wrapped in the lobed, glaucous-blue leaf before it unfurls. Flowers last only 1–2 days in warm weather but are intensely striking. The common name refers to the bright orange-red sap from the rhizome, which was used by many Indigenous peoples as a dye, insect repellent, and medicine — note that this sap is caustic and toxic in quantity. Plants go dormant by midsummer; mass planting with hostas or ferns hides the bare spots.

planting.note: Plant rooted stem cuttings or small division in spring in moist, deeply shaded, acidic, humus-rich soil; partridgeberry roots naturally along its trailing stems and can be divided easily. Avoid planting in bright sun or dry soil.

directions: Plant in moist, acidic, humus-rich soil in deep to partial shade; partridgeberry is a delicate, mat-forming native groundcover of the eastern forest floor with tiny, paired, evergreen leaves marked with a white midrib and charming white tubular flowers in late spring. Each pair of flowers fuses to produce a single bright red berry that persists through winter and is eaten by grouse, turkeys, and other woodland wildlife. The plant is exquisitely scaled for use in woodland terrariums and shade container gardens. It is slow-growing and requires patience, but creates one of the most beautiful and authentic native understory groundcovers for deeply shaded, moist gardens. Excellent companion for mosses, trilliums, and christmas ferns.
